summary: Spolsky challenges Postel's Law—the principle that programs should be liberal in what they accept—arguing that HTML's evolution proved this approach flawed. He contends that stricter APIs and development environments produce more robust software. Using Java's fixed data type sizes as a positive example and citing Russian Field Marshal Suvorov's maxim about hard drills, he advocates for developing under the harshest possible conditions so that software handles real-world edge cases effortlessly.
key_points: Postel's Law ('be liberal in what you accept') led to the mess of HTML parsing and is not a sound engineering principle
Stricter APIs and compilers catch problems early, producing code that works reliably across diverse environments
Java's decision to fix data type sizes was superior to C's loose specification approach
Developers should intentionally use extreme, unusual system configurations during development to surface hidden assumptions
Robustness comes from training under harsh conditions, not from tolerating sloppy inputs
money_quotes: People understand instinctively that the best way for computer programs to communicate with each other is for each of the them to be strict in what they emit, and liberal in what they accept.
I think that the evolution of HTML has proven that this isn't such a great idea.
The stricter the API is about its input, the more likely the code is going to work in funny situations.
A hard drill makes an easy battle.
You want your compiler and your development environment to be as strict as possible.

You want to use French international settings on Chinese Windows 2000 with an absurd color scheme, DVORAK keyboard, trackball, 640×480 VGA mode, and huge ugly fonts on your development system so that you remember to bake in the code that adjusts for all these things.
Then your application will be buff and strong and it will laugh in the face of wimpy problems like people who use commas instead of dots as the decimal.
Ha. I eat commas for breakfast, your code will say, with a Russian accent.
key_insight: Developing under the strictest possible conditions and rejecting permissive input handling produces software that is genuinely robust, not merely tolerant of errors.
